Executes a given bash command in a persistent shell session with optional timeout, ensuring proper handling and security measures.

Be aware: OS: linux, Shell: bash

All commands run in the current working directory by default. Use the `workdir` parameter if you need to run a command in a different directory. AVOID using `cd <directory> && <command>` patterns - use `workdir` instead.

Use `/tmp/opencode` for temporary work outside the workspace. This directory has already been created, already exists, and is pre-approved for external directory access.

IMPORTANT: This tool is for terminal operations like git, npm, docker, etc. DO NOT use it for file operations (reading, writing, editing, searching, finding files) - use the specialized tools for this instead.

Before executing the command, please follow these steps:

1. Directory Verification:
   - If the command will create new directories or files, first use `ls` to verify the parent directory exists and is the correct location
   - For example, before running "mkdir foo/bar", first use `ls foo` to check that "foo" exists and is the intended parent directory

2. Command Execution:
   - Always quote file paths that contain spaces with double quotes (e.g., rm "path with spaces/file.txt")
   - Examples of proper quoting:
     - mkdir "/Users/name/My Documents" (correct)
     - mkdir /Users/name/My Documents (incorrect - will fail)
     - python "/path/with spaces/script.py" (correct)
     - python /path/with spaces/script.py (incorrect - will fail)
   - After ensuring proper quoting, execute the command.
   - Capture the output of the command.

Usage notes:
  - The command argument is required.
  - You can specify an optional timeout in milliseconds. If not specified, commands will time out after 120000ms (2 minutes).
  - It is very helpful if you write a clear, concise description of what this command does in 5-10 words.
  - If the output exceeds 2000 lines or 51200 bytes, it will be truncated and the full output will be written to a file. You can use Read with offset/limit to read specific sections or Grep to search the full content. Do NOT use `head`, `tail`, or other truncation commands to limit output; the full output will already be captured to a file for more precise searching.

  - Avoid using Bash with the `find`, `grep`, `cat`, `head`, `tail`, `sed`, `awk`, or `echo` commands, unless explicitly instructed or when these commands are truly necessary for the task. Instead, always prefer using the dedicated tools for these commands:
    - File search: Use Glob (NOT find or ls)
    - Content search: Use Grep (NOT grep or rg)
    - Read files: Use Read (NOT cat/head/tail)
    - Edit files: Use Edit (NOT sed/awk)
    - Write files: Use Write (NOT echo >/cat <<EOF)
    - Communication: Output text directly (NOT echo/printf)
  - When issuing multiple commands:
    - If the commands are independent and can run in parallel, make multiple Bash tool calls in a single message. For example, if you need to run "git status" and "git diff", send a single message with two Bash tool calls in parallel.
    - If the commands depend on each other and must run sequentially, use a single Bash call with '&&' to chain them together (e.g., `git add . && git commit -m "message" && git push`). For instance, if one operation must complete before another starts (like mkdir before cp, Write before Bash for git operations, or git add before git commit), run these operations sequentially instead.
    - Use ';' only when you need to run commands sequentially but don't care if earlier commands fail
    - DO NOT use newlines to separate commands (newlines are ok in quoted strings)
  - AVOID using `cd <directory> && <command>`. Use the `workdir` parameter to change directories instead.
    <good-example>
    Use workdir="/foo/bar" with command: pytest tests
    </good-example>
    <bad-example>
    cd /foo/bar && pytest tests

# Committing changes with git

Only create commits when requested by the user. If unclear, ask first. When the user asks you to create a new git commit, follow these steps carefully:

Git Safety Protocol:
- NEVER update the git config
- NEVER run destructive/irreversible git commands (like push --force, hard reset, etc) unless the user explicitly requests them
- NEVER skip hooks (--no-verify, --no-gpg-sign, etc) unless the user explicitly requests it
- NEVER run force push to main/master, warn the user if they request it
- Avoid git commit --amend. ONLY use --amend when ALL conditions are met:
  (1) User explicitly requested amend, OR the commit succeeded and pre-commit hooks auto-modified files that need including — verify by checking `git log` that HEAD is the new commit before amending
  (2) HEAD commit was created by you in this conversation (verify: git log -1 --format='%an %ae')
  (3) Commit has NOT been pushed to remote (verify: git status shows "Your branch is ahead")
- CRITICAL: If commit FAILED or was REJECTED by hook, NEVER amend - fix the issue and create a NEW commit
- CRITICAL: If you already pushed to remote, NEVER amend unless user explicitly requests it (requires force push)
- NEVER commit changes unless the user explicitly asks you to. It is VERY IMPORTANT to only commit when explicitly asked, otherwise the user will feel that you are being too proactive.

1. You can call multiple tools in a single response. When multiple independent pieces of information are requested and all commands are likely to succeed, run multiple tool calls in parallel for optimal performance. run the following bash commands in parallel, each using the Bash tool:
  - Run a git status command to see all untracked files.
  - Run a git diff command to see both staged and unstaged changes that will be committed.
  - Run a git log command to see recent commit messages, so that you can follow this repository's commit message style.
2. Analyze all staged changes (both previously staged and newly added) and draft a commit message:
  - Summarize the nature of the changes (eg. new feature, enhancement to an existing feature, bug fix, refactoring, test, docs, etc.). Ensure the message accurately reflects the changes and their purpose (i.e. "add" means a wholly new feature, "update" means an enhancement to an existing feature, "fix" means a bug fix, etc.).
  - Do not commit files that likely contain secrets (.env, credentials.json, etc.). Warn the user if they specifically request to commit those files
  - Draft a concise (1-2 sentences) commit message that focuses on the "why" rather than the "what"
  - Ensure it accurately reflects the changes and their purpose
3. You can call multiple tools in a single response. When multiple independent pieces of information are requested and all commands are likely to succeed, run multiple tool calls in parallel for optimal performance. run the following commands:
   - Add relevant untracked files to the staging area.
   - Create the commit with a message
   - Run git status after the commit completes to verify success.
   Note: git status depends on the commit completing, so run it sequentially after the commit.
4. If the commit fails due to pre-commit hook, fix the issue and create a NEW commit (see amend rules above)

Important notes:
- NEVER run additional commands to read or explore code, besides git bash commands
- NEVER use the TodoWrite or Task tools
- DO NOT push to the remote repository unless the user explicitly asks you to do so
- IMPORTANT: Never use git commands with the -i flag (like git rebase -i or git add -i) since they require interactive input which is not supported.
- If there are no changes to commit (i.e., no untracked files and no modifications), do not create an empty commit

2. Analyze all changes that will be included in the pull request, making sure to look at all relevant commits (NOT just the latest commit, but ALL commits that will be included in the pull request!!!), and draft a pull request summary
3. You can call multiple tools in a single response. When multiple independent pieces of information are requested and all commands are likely to succeed, run multiple tool calls in parallel for optimal performance. run the following commands in parallel:

Use this tool to create and manage a structured task list for your current coding session. This helps you track progress, organize complex tasks, and demonstrate thoroughness to the user.
It also helps the user understand the progress of the task and overall progress of their requests.

## When to Use This Tool
Use this tool proactively in these scenarios:

1. Complex multistep tasks - When a task requires 3 or more distinct steps or actions
2. Non-trivial and complex tasks - Tasks that require careful planning or multiple operations
3. User explicitly requests todo list - When the user directly asks you to use the todo list
4. User provides multiple tasks - When users provide a list of things to be done (numbered or comma-separated)
5. After receiving new instructions - Immediately capture user requirements as todos. Feel free to edit the todo list based on new information.
6. After completing a task - Mark it complete and add any new follow-up tasks
7. When you start working on a new task, mark the todo as in_progress. Ideally you should only have one todo as in_progress at a time. Complete existing tasks before starting new ones.

## When NOT to Use This Tool

Skip using this tool when:
1. There is only a single, straightforward task
2. The task is trivial and tracking it provides no organizational benefit
3. The task can be completed in less than 3 trivial steps
4. The task is purely conversational or informational

NOTE that you should not use this tool if there is only one trivial task to do. In this case you are better off just doing the task directly.

## Examples of When to Use the Todo List

<example>
User: I want to add a dark mode toggle to the application settings. Make sure you run the tests and build when you're done!
Assistant: I'll help add a dark mode toggle to your application settings. Let me create a todo list to track this implementation.
*Creates todo list with the following items:*
1. Create dark mode toggle component in Settings page
2. Add dark mode state management (context/store)
3. Implement CSS-in-JS styles for dark theme
4. Update existing components to support theme switching
5. Run tests and build process, addressing any failures or errors that occur
*Begins working on the first task*

<reasoning>
The assistant used the todo list because:
1. Adding dark mode is a multi-step feature requiring UI, state management, and styling changes
2. The user explicitly requested tests and build be run afterward
3. The assistant inferred that tests and build need to pass by adding "Ensure tests and build succeed" as the final task
</reasoning>
</example>

<example>
User: Help me rename the function getCwd to getCurrentWorkingDirectory across my project
Assistant: Let me first search through your codebase to find all occurrences of 'getCwd'.
*Uses grep or search tools to locate all instances of getCwd in the codebase*
Assistant: I've found 15 instances of 'getCwd' across 8 different files. Let me create a todo list to track these changes.
*Creates todo list with specific items for each file that needs updating*

<reasoning>
The assistant used the todo list because:
1. First, the assistant searched to understand the scope of the task
2. Upon finding multiple occurrences across different files, it determined this was a complex task with multiple steps
3. The todo list helps ensure every instance is tracked and updated systematically
4. This approach prevents missing any occurrences and maintains code consistency
</reasoning>
</example>


<example>
User: I need to implement these features for my e-commerce site: user registration, product catalog, shopping cart, and checkout flow.
Assistant: I'll help implement these features. First, let's add all the features to the todo list.
*Creates a todo list breaking down each feature into specific tasks based on the project architecture*
Assistant: Let's start with user registration. This will involve creating database models, API endpoints, and frontend forms that integrate with your existing authentication system.

<reasoning>
The assistant used the todo list because:
1. The user provided multiple complex features to implement in a comma separated list
2. The todo list helps organize these large features into manageable tasks
3. This approach allows for tracking progress across the entire implementation
</reasoning>
</example>

<example>
User: Can you help optimize my React application? It's rendering slowly and has performance issues.</user>
Assistant: I'll help optimize your React application. First, let me examine your codebase to identify potential performance bottlenecks.
*Reviews component structure, render patterns, state management, and data fetching*
Assistant: After analyzing your codebase, I've identified several performance issues. Let me create a todo list to track our optimization efforts.
*Creates todo list with items like: 1) Implement memoization for expensive calculations in ProductList, 2) Add virtualization for long lists in Dashboard, 3) Optimize image loading in Gallery component, 4) Fix state update loops in ShoppingCart, 5) Review bundle size and implement code splitting*
Let's start by implementing memoization for the expensive calculations in your ProductList component.</assistant>

<reasoning>
The assistant used the todo list because:
1. First, the assistant examined the codebase to identify specific performance issues
2. Based on this analysis, it identified multiple optimization opportunities
3. Performance optimization is a non-trivial task requiring multiple steps
4. The todo list helps methodically track improvements across different components
5. This systematic approach ensures all performance bottlenecks are addressed
</reasoning>
</example>

## Examples of When NOT to Use the Todo List

<example>
User: How do I print 'Hello World' in Python?
Assistant: In Python, you can print "Hello World" with this simple code:

python
print("Hello World")

This will output the text "Hello World" to the console when executed.</assistant>

<reasoning>
The assistant did not use the todo list because this is a single, trivial task that can be completed in one step. There's no need to track multiple tasks or steps for such a straightforward request.
</reasoning>
</example>

<example>
User: What does the git status command do?
Assistant: The git status command shows the current state of your working directory and staging area. It displays which changes have been staged, which haven't, and which files aren't being tracked by Git. This is a fundamental command for understanding the current state of your repository before making commits.

<reasoning>
The assistant did not use the todo list because this is an informational request with no actual coding task to complete. The user is simply asking for an explanation, not for the assistant to perform multiple steps or tasks.
</reasoning>
</example>

<example>
User: Can you add a comment to the calculateTotal function to explain what it does?
Assistant: Sure, let me add a comment to the calculateTotal function to explain what it does.
* Uses the Edit tool to add a comment to the calculateTotal function *

<reasoning>
The assistant did not use the todo list because this is a single, straightforward task confined to one location in the code. Adding a comment doesn't require tracking multiple steps or systematic organization.

Assistant: I'll run the npm install command for you.

The command completed successfully. Here's the output:

All dependencies have been installed according to your package.json file.

<reasoning>
The assistant did not use the todo list because this is a single command execution with immediate results. There are no multiple steps to track or organize, making the todo list unnecessary for this straightforward task.
</reasoning>
</example>

## Task States and Management

1. **Task States**: Use these states to track progress:
   - pending: Task not yet started
   - in_progress: Currently working on (limit to ONE task at a time)
   - completed: Task finished successfully
   - cancelled: Task no longer needed

2. **Task Management**:
   - Update task status in real-time as you work
   - Mark tasks complete IMMEDIATELY after finishing (don't batch completions)
   - Only have ONE task in_progress at any time
   - Complete current tasks before starting new ones
   - Cancel tasks that become irrelevant

3. **Task Breakdown**:
   - Create specific, actionable items
   - Break complex tasks into smaller, manageable steps
   - Use clear, descriptive task names

When in doubt, use this tool. Being proactive with task management demonstrates attentiveness and ensures you complete all requirements successfully.

data.content:
# Write Cypher

## Overview

Use this skill for VAST DB Cypher query patterns. Prefer reusable templates, and construct new queries when no template matches the analysis need.

## Workflow

1. Read `references/query-templates.md`. If a template matches the task, reuse it as-is.
2. If no template matches, construct a new Cypher query using the VAST DB schema and the closest related template as guidance.

## Query Strategy

- Load `understand-mlir-schema` when query construction or result interpretation depends on schema details.
- Use small exploratory queries with `LIMIT` when schema details such as operation names, properties, node and relationship labels are uncertain.
- When starting from a source location, first map it to operation `uid`s. Use those `uid`s as stable handles for follow-up queries such as enclosing functions, call relationships, and def-use data flow.
- Return only properties needed for the analysis. Common fields are node `uid`, relationship `idx`, operation `name`, `location`, `func_name`, `fs_raw_name`, `varName`, `className`, type `type`, and attribute `value`.
- Order indexed relationships by `idx`.

## Query Rules

- Generate read-only queries by default. Do not use `CREATE`, `MERGE`, `SET`, `DELETE`, or `REMOVE`.
- Use parameter placeholders such as `$func_name`, `$op_uid`, `$file_name`, `$line`, and `$callee`.
- Do not invent node and relationship labels, properties, or operation names. Verify unfamiliar schema details with `understand-mlir-schema` or exploratory queries.

## Common Patterns

Read `references/query-templates.md` for reusable Cypher templates.

Use this before writing Cypher queries against VAST DB. For query patterns, load write-cypher.

# Understand MLIR Schema

## Overview

This skill describes the VAST DB schema. Use it to check node labels, relationship types, and properties before writing or interpreting Cypher queries. Do not guess labels or relationships; look them up here.

## Core Schema

### Node Labels

- `OPERATIONS` — MLIR operations. Properties include `uid`, `name`, `func_name`, `fs_raw_name`, `varName`, `mod_name`, and `location`. The `location` field has the form `['F', file, line, column]`.
- `VALUES` — SSA values. Only property is `uid`.
- `INTATTRS` — Integer attributes. Properties: `uid`, `value` (int), `attr_type` (int).
- `FLOATATTRS` — Float attributes. Properties: `uid`, `value` (float), `attr_type` (int).
- `STRINGATTRS` — String attributes. Properties: `uid`, `value` (string), `attr_type` (int).
- `TYPES` — MLIR types. Properties: `uid`, `type` (type mnemonic), `has_dl` (bool), `bw` (int bitwidth), `align` (int), `record_name` (string, for hl.record only).
- `REGIONS` — MLIR regions. Properties: `uid`, `regType` (bool: true for GraphRegion, false for SSACFG).
- `BLOCKS` — MLIR blocks. Only property is `uid`.
- `VTABLES` — Virtual tables. Properties: `uid`, `vtable_name`, `components_flat`, `components_sizes`.
- `VTTS` — VTT (virtual table table). Properties: `uid`, `vtt_name`, `components_flat`.

### Key Relationships

Operation operands and results:
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:ARGVALUE {idx}]->(:VALUES)` — operation consumes a value as operand
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:RESVALUE {idx}]->(:VALUES)` — operation produces a value as result
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:ARGATTR {idx}]->(:INTATTRS|FLOATATTRS|STRINGATTRS)` — operation has an attribute
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:ARGBLOCK {idx}]->(:BLOCKS)` — operation references a block (e.g., branch targets)
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:ARGTYPE {idx}]->(:TYPES)` — operation has a type operand

Containment (operation bodies):
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:CHILDREGION {idx}]->(:REGIONS)` — operation contains a region
- `(:REGIONS)-[:CHILDBLOCK {idx}]->(:BLOCKS)` — region contains a block
- `(:BLOCKS)-[:CHILDINST {idx}]->(:OPERATIONS)` — block contains an instruction (operation)
- `(:BLOCKS)-[:BLOCKARG {idx}]->(:VALUES)` — block has a block argument

Type system:
- `(:VALUES)-[:TYPE]->(:TYPES)` — value has a type
- `(:TYPES)-[:ARG {idx}]->(:TYPES)` — composite type has component types
- `(:INTATTRS|:FLOATATTRS)-[:TYPE]->(:TYPES)` — non-string attribute has a type

Function and variable types:
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:FUNCTYPE]->(:TYPES)` — global function's type signature
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:VARTYPE]->(:TYPES)` — global variable's type

Virtual tables (only from `builtin.module` operations):
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:ARGVTABLE]->(:VTABLES)` — module has a vtable
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:ARGVTT]->(:VTTS)` — module has a VTT

## References

- `references/schema-mlir-graph.md` — canonical node labels, relationships, properties, constraints, and example queries.
- `references/operations-catalog.md` — every operation name (`hl.func`, `hl.call`, `ll.load`, etc.) with its specific properties and relationships.
- `references/types-catalog.md` — every type mnemonic (`hl.int`, `i32`, `core.fn`, etc.) with its properties and subtype structure.

parts.text:
Let me compare the bad and good versions to identify the fix. The key differences are:

1. **Line 854 (good.c) vs bad.c**: In `do_test`, the good version adds `dp->error_code = VERBOSE;` before `setjmp`, and uses `dp->error_code` in the longjmp handling instead of `ret`. The bad version's `do_test` returns `ret` (the setjmp return value), while the good version returns `dp->error_code`.

2. **Lines 964-969 (good.c) vs bad.c (line 963-964)**: In `main`, the good version adds cleanup calls:
   - Bad: `if (ret > QUIET) return 99;`
   - Good: `if (ret > QUIET) { display_clean(&d); display_destroy(&d); return 99; }`

Wait, let me re-read more carefully.

Actually, looking at the bad version `do_test`:
```c
int ret = setjmp(dp->error_return);
if (ret == 0) {
    test_one_file(dp, file);
    return 0;
} else if (ret < ERRORS)
    display_log(dp, INTERNAL_ERROR, "unexpected return code %d", ret);
return ret;
```

And in the good version `do_test`:
```c
dp->error_code = VERBOSE;
if (setjmp(dp->error_return) == 0) {
    test_one_file(dp, file);
    return 0;
} else if (dp->error_code < ERRORS)
    display_log(dp, INTERNAL_ERROR, "unexpected return code %d", dp->error_code);
return dp->error_code;
```

Hmm, this is a different approach. In the bad version, the `ret` from `setjmp` is the value passed to `longjmp`. Looking at `display_error` and `display_log`:

`display_log` (not shown but referenced) calls `longjmp` with the level value. The `setjmp` returns this value.

In the bad version, `do_test` returns this level directly. In `main`:
```c
int ret = do_test(&d, argv[i]);
if (ret > QUIET) /* abort on user or internal error */
    return 99;
```

So the issue is: when a longjmp occurs due to an error, `do_test` returns the error level, and if it's > QUIET, main returns 99 without calling `display_clean(&d)` or `display_destroy(&d)`. These cleanup functions would normally free the memory (including the png_struct allocated by `png_create_read_struct`).

In the good version, the fix is:
```c
if (ret > QUIET) /* abort on user or internal error */
{
    display_clean(&d);
    display_destroy(&d);
    return 99;
}
```

The fix adds `display_clean(&d)` and `display_destroy(&d)` before `return 99;` in `main`.

Now, both explanations say the same thing: that memory allocated by `png_create_read_struct` is not freed when `return 99;` is hit in main, bypassing cleanup.

However, both explanations have some issues:

1. **Baseline** says: "When an error occurs (e.g., from display_log), a longjmp transfers control back to do_test, which returns the error code to main. If the error code exceeds QUIET, main returns 99 immediately (line 964), bypassing the cleanup calls display_clean (line 990) and display_destroy (line 994)."

   This is correct but mentions line 990 and 994 which in the `good` version are at lines 995 and 999. In the bad version, `display_clean` is at line 990 and `display_destroy` at line 994. This is accurate.

   Baseline also mentions: "memory allocated by png_create_read_struct (line 582) is not released when the program exits early via return 99 (line 964). In the read_png function, a png_struct is allocated and stored in dp->read_pp (line 582), and later moved to dp->original_pp (line 635) after update_display."

   This is accurate.

2. **VastDB** says: "In the read_png function at line 582, png_create_read_struct allocates memory for a PNG read structure and stores the pointer in dp->read_pp. This memory should be freed using png_destroy_read_struct, but no such deallocation function is ever called in the codebase."

   However, this is not quite right. The memory IS freed by `display_destroy` and `display_clean_read` (called at the start of `read_png`). The issue is specifically that `return 99;` in `main` bypasses those cleanup functions. So claiming "no such deallocation function is ever called in the codebase" is incorrect - the deallocation functions exist and are called in the normal code path, just not on this specific early return path.

   VastDB also says: "When an unknown option is passed (line 946-947), it returns 99 directly without cleanup."
   
   This is technically also a memory leak but is less relevant to the CVE since that happens before any allocation occurs (before any file is processed).

Both explanations correctly identify the root cause as a memory leak when `return 99;` is hit at line 964 in main, bypassing cleanup. However, VastDB has the inaccurate statement about "no such deallocation function is ever called in the codebase."

Let me look more carefully at the real fix. The fix in `good.c` is:

```c
if (ret > QUIET) /* abort on user or internal error */
{
    display_clean(&d);
    display_destroy(&d);
    return 99;
}
```

This adds `display_clean(&d)` and `display_destroy(&d)` before `return 99;`. That's the actual fix.

Now let me look at `do_test` changes too. The good version also changes `do_test`:
- Bad: `int ret = setjmp(...); if (ret == 0) ... else if (ret < ERRORS) ... return ret;`
- Good: `dp->error_code = VERBOSE; if (setjmp(...) == 0) ... else if (dp->error_code < ERRORS) ... return dp->error_code;`

This change in `do_test` is also important - it stores the error code in `dp->error_code` rather than relying on the `setjmp` return value.

Wait, but the main fix is actually simpler. Let me check what `display_log` does. It presumably stores the level in `dp` somewhere and then does `longjmp`. But the original code uses the return value of `setjmp` which is the value passed to `longjmp`. 

Actually, looking more carefully at the bad `do_test`, `display_log` (a function declared but not shown here) presumably does:
1. Sets `dp->results |= (1U << level)`
2. If level >= LIBPNG_ERROR, calls `longjmp(dp->error_return, level)`

So when `longjmp` is called with the level, `setjmp` returns that level, and `do_test` returns it. Then `main` checks if `ret > QUIET`.

In the good version, the key change is adding `display_clean` and `display_destroy` before `return 99;` in main. This ensures memory is cleaned up before the early exit.

Let me also look at whether there are other changes in the good version's `do_test`. In the good version:
- `dp->error_code = VERBOSE;` initializes before setjmp
- Uses `dp->error_code` instead of the raw setjmp return value
- This implies that `display_log` or some related function stores the level in `dp->error_code` before doing `longjmp`

So both fixes together: (1) track error code properly in the struct, and (2) add cleanup calls before early return.

Now evaluating the explanations:

**Baseline**: Correctly identifies that memory allocated by `png_create_read_struct` at line 582 leaks when `return 99;` at line 964 bypasses `display_clean` (line 990) and `display_destroy` (line 994). Correctly identifies the longjmp control flow path. No unsupported speculation. Score: 9/10 (minor quibble: doesn't mention the `do_test` struct member change, and line numbers refer to bad.c).

**VastDB**: Correctly identifies memory leak via `png_create_read_struct` and `return 99`. However, makes a false claim: "no such deallocation function is ever called in the codebase" — this is wrong because `display_clean` and `display_destroy` do exist and are called in normal flow. Also mentions the unknown option path (line 947) which is less relevant since no allocation happens before that point (it's in main before any file processing). Score: 6/10 (one substantively incorrect claim drags it down).

Actually, let me reconsider. The VastDB says:
"This memory should be freed using png_destroy_read_struct, but no such deallocation function is ever called in the codebase."

This is a clear error. `display_destroy` and `display_clean_read` do call the appropriate deallocation. The issue is just that `return 99;` at line 964 bypasses those calls.

Also, the VastDB traces path through test_one_file return paths at lines 717-718 and 729-730, but those returns in test_one_file don't go through `return 99;` in main — they just return from test_one_file back to do_test which returns 0. The actual leak path is via longjmp → do_test returning error level > QUIET → main's `return 99;`.

So VastDB has a mixed trigger path that conflates different return mechanisms. The baseline is cleaner and more accurate.

Let me finalize my judgement.

Based on my analysis of the code and VAST DB queries, I can now provide the complete analysis:

## Root Cause

The vulnerability is a CWE-401 Missing Release of Memory after Effective Lifetime. In the `read_png` function at line 582, `png_create_read_struct` allocates memory for a PNG read structure and stores the pointer in `dp->read_pp`. This memory should be freed using `png_destroy_read_struct`, but no such deallocation function is ever called in the codebase. When the program exits via `return 99;` at either line 947 (unknown command-line option) or line 964 (test failure return value > QUIET), the allocated png_struct memory is never freed, causing a memory leak.

## Trigger Path

1. `bad.c:868` `main`
   - Entry point that processes command-line arguments and calls `do_test`. When an unknown option is passed (line 946-947), it returns 99 directly without cleanup.

2. `bad.c:850` `do_test`
   - Sets up error handling using `setjmp`/`longjmp`, then calls `test_one_file`. If a test returns a value > QUIET (checked at line 963-964), it returns 99 without cleanup.

3. `bad.c:698` `test_one_file`
   - Calls `update_display` to read and validate the PNG file. On certain error conditions (line 717-718, 729-730), it returns early without freeing memory.

4. `bad.c:623` `update_display`
   - Calls `read_png` to perform the initial PNG read with no transforms, then moves the read pointers to `original_pp`/`original_ip` fields.

360: PNG_FUNCTION(png_voidp /* PRIVATE */,
361: png_malloc_base,(png_const_structrp png_ptr, png_alloc_size_t size),
362:     PNG_EMPTY)
363: {
364:    /* Moved to png_malloc_base from png_malloc_default in 1.6.0; the DOS
365:     * allocators have also been removed in 1.6.0, so any 16-bit system now has
366:     * to implement a user memory handler.  This checks to be sure it isn't
367:     * called with big numbers.
368:     */
369: #  ifdef PNG_MAX_MALLOC_64K
370:       /* This is support for legacy systems which had segmented addressing
371:        * limiting the maximum allocation size to 65536.  It takes precedence
372:        * over PNG_SIZE_MAX which is set to 65535 on true 16-bit systems.
373:        *
374:        * TODO: libpng-1.8: finally remove both cases.
375:        */
376:       if (size > 65536U) return NULL;
377: #  endif
378: 
379:    /* This is checked too because the system malloc call below takes a (size_t).
380:     */
381:    if (size > PNG_SIZE_MAX) return NULL;
382: 
383: #  ifdef PNG_USER_MEM_SUPPORTED
384:       if (png_ptr != NULL && png_ptr->malloc_fn != NULL)
385:          return (png_voidp)png_ptr->malloc_fn(png_constcast(png_structrp,png_ptr), size);
386: #  else
387:       PNG_UNUSED(png_ptr)
388: #  endif
389: 
390:    /* Use the system malloc */
391:    return malloc((size_t)/*SAFE*/size); /* checked for truncation above */
392: }
393: 
394: PNG_FUNCTION(png_voidp,PNGAPI
395: png_malloc_warn,(png_const_structrp png_ptr, png_alloc_size_t size),
396:     PNG_ALLOCATED)
397: {
398:    if (png_ptr != NULL)
399:    {
400:       png_voidp ret = png_malloc_base(png_ptr, size);
401: 
402:       if (ret != NULL)
403:          return ret;
404: 
405:       png_warning(png_ptr, "Out of memory");
406:    }
407: 
408:    return NULL;
409: }

411: PNG_FUNCTION(png_structp /* PRIVATE */,
412: png_create_png_struct,(png_const_charp user_png_ver, png_voidp error_ptr,
413:     png_error_ptr error_fn, png_error_ptr warn_fn, png_voidp mem_ptr,
414:     png_malloc_ptr malloc_fn, png_free_ptr free_fn),PNG_ALLOCATED)
415: {
416:    png_struct create_struct;
417: #  ifdef PNG_SETJMP_SUPPORTED
418:       jmp_buf create_jmp_buf;
419: #  endif
420: 
421:    /* This temporary stack-allocated structure is used to provide a place to
422:     * build enough context to allow the user provided memory allocator (if any)
423:     * to be called.
424:     */
425:    memset(&create_struct, 0, (sizeof create_struct));
426: 
427: #  ifdef PNG_USER_LIMITS_SUPPORTED
428:       create_struct.user_width_max = PNG_USER_WIDTH_MAX;
429:       create_struct.user_height_max = PNG_USER_HEIGHT_MAX;
430: 
431: #     ifdef PNG_USER_CHUNK_CACHE_MAX
432:       create_struct.user_chunk_cache_max = PNG_USER_CHUNK_CACHE_MAX;
433: #     endif
434: 
435: #     if PNG_USER_CHUNK_MALLOC_MAX > 0 /* default to compile-time limit */
436:       create_struct.user_chunk_malloc_max = PNG_USER_CHUNK_MALLOC_MAX;
437: 
438:       /* No compile-time limit, so initialize to the system limit: */
439: #     elif defined PNG_MAX_MALLOC_64K /* legacy system limit */
440:       create_struct.user_chunk_malloc_max = 65536U;
441: 
442: #     else /* modern system limit SIZE_MAX (C99) */
443:       create_struct.user_chunk_malloc_max = PNG_SIZE_MAX;
444: #     endif
445: #  endif
446: 
447:    /* The following two API calls simply set fields in png_struct, so it is safe
448:     * to do them now even though error handling is not yet set up.
449:     */
450: #  ifdef PNG_USER_MEM_SUPPORTED
451:       png_set_mem_fn(&create_struct, mem_ptr, malloc_fn, free_fn);
452: #  else
453:       PNG_UNUSED(mem_ptr)
454:       PNG_UNUSED(malloc_fn)
455:       PNG_UNUSED(free_fn)
456: #  endif
457: 
458:    /* (*error_fn) can return control to the caller after the error_ptr is set,
459:     * this will result in a memory leak unless the error_fn does something
460:     * extremely sophisticated.  The design lacks merit but is implicit in the
461:     * API.
462:     */
463:    png_set_error_fn(&create_struct, error_ptr, error_fn, warn_fn);
464: 
465: #  ifdef PNG_SETJMP_SUPPORTED
466:       if (!setjmp(create_jmp_buf))
467: #  endif
468:       {
469: #  ifdef PNG_SETJMP_SUPPORTED
470:          /* Temporarily fake out the longjmp information until we have
471:           * successfully completed this function.  This only works if we have
472:           * setjmp() support compiled in, but it is safe - this stuff should
473:           * never happen.
474:           */
475:          create_struct.jmp_buf_ptr = &create_jmp_buf;
476:          create_struct.jmp_buf_size = 0; /*stack allocation*/
477:          create_struct.longjmp_fn = longjmp;
478: #  endif
479:          /* Call the general version checker (shared with read and write code):
480:           */
481:          if (png_user_version_check(&create_struct, user_png_ver) != 0)
482:          {
483:             png_structrp png_ptr = png_voidcast(png_structrp,
484:                 png_malloc_warn(&create_struct, (sizeof *png_ptr)));
485: 
486:             if (png_ptr != NULL)
487:             {
488:                /* png_ptr->zstream holds a back-pointer to the png_struct, so
489:                 * this can only be done now:
490:                 */
491:                create_struct.zstream.zalloc = png_zalloc;
492:                create_struct.zstream.zfree = png_zfree;
493:                create_struct.zstream.opaque = png_ptr;
494: 
495: #              ifdef PNG_SETJMP_SUPPORTED
496:                /* Eliminate the local error handling: */
497:                create_struct.jmp_buf_ptr = NULL;
498:                create_struct.jmp_buf_size = 0;
499:                create_struct.longjmp_fn = 0;
500: #              endif
501: 
502:                *png_ptr = create_struct;
503: 
504:                /* This is the successful return point */
505:                return png_ptr;
506:             }
507:          }
508:       }
509: 
510:    /* A longjmp because of a bug in the application storage allocator or a
511:     * simple failure to allocate the png_struct.
512:     */
513:    return NULL;
514: }

612:       fprintf(stderr, "%.4x %2d (%3lu bytes):", transforms, png_get_bit_depth(pp,ip), (unsigned long)rb);

614:       for (cb=0; cb<rb; ++cb)
615:          fputc(c, stderr), fprintf(stderr, "%.2x", pr[cb]), c='.';

622: static void
623: update_display(struct display *dp)
624:    /* called once after the first read to update all the info, original_pp and
625:     * original_ip must have been filled in.
626:     */
627: {
628:    png_structp pp;
629:    png_infop   ip;
630: 
631:    /* Now perform the initial read with a 0 transform. */
632:    read_png(dp, &dp->original_file, "original read", 0/*no transform*/);
633: 
634:    /* Move the result to the 'original' fields */
635:    dp->original_pp = pp = dp->read_pp, dp->read_pp = NULL;
636:    dp->original_ip = ip = dp->read_ip, dp->read_ip = NULL;
637: 
638:    dp->original_rowbytes = png_get_rowbytes(pp, ip);
639:    if (dp->original_rowbytes == 0)
640:       display_log(dp, LIBPNG_BUG, "png_get_rowbytes returned 0");
641: 
642:    dp->chunks = png_get_valid(pp, ip, 0xffffffff);
643:    if ((dp->chunks & PNG_INFO_IDAT) == 0) /* set by png_read_png */
644:       display_log(dp, LIBPNG_BUG, "png_read_png did not set IDAT flag");
645: 
646:    dp->original_rows = png_get_rows(pp, ip);
647:    if (dp->original_rows == NULL)
648:       display_log(dp, LIBPNG_BUG, "png_read_png did not create row buffers");
649: 
650:    if (!png_get_IHDR(pp, ip,
651:       &dp->width, &dp->height, &dp->bit_depth, &dp->color_type,
652:       &dp->interlace_method, &dp->compression_method, &dp->filter_method))
653:       display_log(dp, LIBPNG_BUG, "png_get_IHDR failed");
654: 
655:    /* 'active' transforms are discovered based on the original image format;
656:     * running one active transform can activate others.  At present the code
657:     * does not attempt to determine the closure.
658:     */
659:    {
660:       png_uint_32 chunks = dp->chunks;
661:       int active = 0, inactive = 0;
662:       int ct = dp->color_type;
663:       int bd = dp->bit_depth;
664:       unsigned int i;
665: 
666:       for (i=0; i<TTABLE_SIZE; ++i)
667:       {
668:          if (transform_info[i].name != NULL)
669:          {
670:             int transform = transform_info[i].transform;
671: 
672:             if ((transform_info[i].valid_chunks == 0 ||
673:                   (transform_info[i].valid_chunks & chunks) != 0) &&
674:                (transform_info[i].color_mask_required & ct) ==
675:                   transform_info[i].color_mask_required &&
676:                (transform_info[i].color_mask_absent & ct) == 0 &&
677:                (transform_info[i].bit_depths & bd) != 0 &&
678:                (transform_info[i].when & TRANSFORM_R) != 0)
679:                active |= transform;
680: 
681:             else if ((transform_info[i].when & TRANSFORM_R) != 0)
682:                inactive |= transform;
683:          }
684:       }
685: 
686:       /* Some transforms appear multiple times in the table; the 'active' status
687:        * is the logical OR of these and the inactive status must be adjusted to
688:        * take this into account.
689:        */
690:       inactive &= ~active;
691: 
692:       dp->active_transforms = active;
693:       dp->ignored_transforms = inactive; /* excluding write-only transforms */
694:    }

697: static void
698: test_one_file(struct display *dp, const char *filename)
699: {
700:    /* First cache the file and update the display original file
701:     * information for the new file.
702:     */
703:    dp->operation = "cache file";
704:    dp->transforms = 0;
705:    display_cache_file(dp, filename);
706:    update_display(dp);
707: 
708:    /* First test: if there are options that should be ignored for this file
709:     * verify that they really are ignored.
710:     */
711:    if (dp->ignored_transforms != 0)
712:    {
713:       read_png(dp, &dp->original_file, "ignored transforms",
714:          dp->ignored_transforms);
715: 
716:       /* The result should be identical to the original_rows */
717:       if (!compare_read(dp, 0/*transforms applied*/))
718:          return; /* no point testing more */
719:    }
720: 
721: #ifdef PNG_WRITE_PNG_SUPPORTED
722:    /* Second test: write the original PNG data out to a new file (to test the
723:     * write side) then read the result back in and make sure that it hasn't
724:     * changed.
725:     */
726:    dp->operation = "write";
727:    write_png(dp, dp->original_ip, 0/*transforms*/);
728:    read_png(dp, &dp->written_file, NULL, 0/*transforms*/);
729:    if (!compare_read(dp, 0/*transforms applied*/))
730:       return;
731: #endif
732: 
733:    /* Third test: the active options.  Test each in turn, or, with the
734:     * EXHAUSTIVE option, test all possible combinations.
735:     */
736:    {
737:       /* Use unsigned int here because the code below to increment through all
738:        * the possibilities exhaustively has to use a compare and that must be
739:        * unsigned, because some transforms are negative on a 16-bit system.
740:        */
741:       unsigned int active = dp->active_transforms;
742:       int exhaustive = (dp->options & EXHAUSTIVE) != 0;
743:       unsigned int current = first_transform(active);
744:       unsigned int bad_transforms = 0;
745:       unsigned int bad_combo = ~0U;    /* bitwise AND of failing transforms */
746:       unsigned int bad_combo_list = 0; /* bitwise OR of failures */
747: 
748:       for (;;)
749:       {
750:          read_png(dp, &dp->original_file, "active transforms", current);
751: 
752:          /* If this involved any irreversible transformations then if we write
753:           * it out with just the reversible transformations and read it in again
754:           * with the same transforms we should get the same thing.  At present
755:           * this isn't done - it just seems like a waste of time and it would
756:           * require two sets of read png_struct/png_info.
757:           *
758:           * If there were no irreversible transformations then if we write it
759:           * out and read it back in again (without the reversible transforms)
760:           * we should get back to the place where we started.
761:           */
762: #ifdef PNG_WRITE_PNG_SUPPORTED
763:          if ((current & write_transforms) == current)
764:          {
765:             /* All transforms reversible: write the PNG with the transformations
766:              * reversed, then read it back in with no transformations.  The
767:              * result should be the same as the original apart from the loss of
768:              * low order bits because of the SHIFT/sBIT transform.
769:              */
770:             dp->operation = "reversible transforms";
771:             write_png(dp, dp->read_ip, current);
772: 
773:             /* And if this is read back in, because all the transformations were
774:              * reversible, the result should be the same.
775:              */
776:             read_png(dp, &dp->written_file, NULL, 0);
777:             if (!compare_read(dp, current/*for the SHIFT/sBIT transform*/))
778:             {
779:                /* This set of transforms failed.  If a single bit is set - if
780:                 * there is just one transform - don't include this in further
781:                 * 'exhaustive' tests.  Notice that each transform is tested on
782:                 * its own before testing combos in the exhaustive case.
783:                 */
784:                if (is_combo(current))
785:                {
786:                   bad_combo &= current;
787:                   bad_combo_list |= current;
788:                }
789: 
790:                else
791:                   bad_transforms |= current;
792:             }
793:          }
794: #endif
795: 
796:          /* Now move to the next transform */
797:          if (exhaustive) /* all combinations */
798:          {
799:             unsigned int next = current;
800: 
801:             do
802:             {
803:                if (next == read_transforms) /* Everything tested */
804:                   goto combo;
805: 
806:                ++next;
807:             }  /* skip known bad combos if the relevant option is set; skip
808:                 * combos involving known bad single transforms in all cases.
809:                 */
810:             while (  (next & read_transforms) <= current
811:                   || (next & active) == 0 /* skip cases that do nothing */
812:                   || (next & bad_transforms) != 0
813:                   || skip_transform(dp, next));
814: 
815:             assert((next & read_transforms) == next);
816:             current = next;
817:          }
818: 
819:          else /* one at a time */
820:          {
821:             active &= ~current;
822: 
823:             if (active == 0)
824:                goto combo;
825: 
826:             current = first_transform(active);
827:          }
828:       }
829: 
830: combo:
831:       if (dp->options & FIND_BAD_COMBOS)
832:       {
833:          /* bad_combos identifies the combos that occur in all failing cases;
834:           * bad_combo_list identifies transforms that do not prevent the
835:           * failure.
836:           */
837:          if (bad_combo != ~0U)
838:             printf("%s[0x%x]: PROBLEM: 0x%x[0x%x] ANTIDOTE: 0x%x\n",
839:                dp->filename, active, bad_combo, bad_combo_list,
840:                rw_transforms & ~bad_combo_list);
841: 
842:          else
843:             printf("%s: no %sbad combos found\n", dp->filename,
844:                (dp->options & SKIP_BUGS) ? "additional " : "");
845:       }
846:    }
847: }

849: static int
850: do_test(struct display *dp, const char *file)
851:    /* Exists solely to isolate the setjmp clobbers */
852: {
853:    int ret = setjmp(dp->error_return);
854: 
855:    if (ret == 0)
856:    {
857:       test_one_file(dp, file);
858:       return 0;
859:    }
860: 
861:    else if (ret < ERRORS) /* shouldn't longjmp on warnings */
862:       display_log(dp, INTERNAL_ERROR, "unexpected return code %d", ret);
863: 
864:    return ret;
865: }

361: PNG_FUNCTION(png_voidp /* PRIVATE */,
362: png_malloc_base,(png_const_structrp png_ptr, png_alloc_size_t size),
363:     PNG_EMPTY)
364: {
365:    /* Moved to png_malloc_base from png_malloc_default in 1.6.0; the DOS
366:     * allocators have also been removed in 1.6.0, so any 16-bit system now has
367:     * to implement a user memory handler.  This checks to be sure it isn't
368:     * called with big numbers.
369:     */
370: #  ifdef PNG_MAX_MALLOC_64K
371:       /* This is support for legacy systems which had segmented addressing
372:        * limiting the maximum allocation size to 65536.  It takes precedence
373:        * over PNG_SIZE_MAX which is set to 65535 on true 16-bit systems.
374:        *
375:        * TODO: libpng-1.8: finally remove both cases.
376:        */
377:       if (size > 65536U) return NULL;
378: #  endif
379: 
380:    /* This is checked too because the system malloc call below takes a (size_t).
381:     */
382:    if (size > PNG_SIZE_MAX) return NULL;
383: 
384: #  ifdef PNG_USER_MEM_SUPPORTED
385:       if (png_ptr != NULL && png_ptr->malloc_fn != NULL)
386:          return (png_voidp)png_ptr->malloc_fn(png_constcast(png_structrp,png_ptr), size);
387: #  else
388:       PNG_UNUSED(png_ptr)
389: #  endif
390: 
391:    /* Use the system malloc */
392:    return malloc((size_t)/*SAFE*/size); /* checked for truncation above */
393: }

395: PNG_FUNCTION(png_voidp,PNGAPI
396: png_malloc_warn,(png_const_structrp png_ptr, png_alloc_size_t size),
397:     PNG_ALLOCATED)
398: {
399:    if (png_ptr != NULL)
400:    {
401:       png_voidp ret = png_malloc_base(png_ptr, size);
402: 
403:       if (ret != NULL)
404:          return ret;
405: 
406:       png_warning(png_ptr, "Out of memory");
407:    }
408: 
409:    return NULL;
410: }

412: PNG_FUNCTION(png_structp /* PRIVATE */,
413: png_create_png_struct,(png_const_charp user_png_ver, png_voidp error_ptr,
414:     png_error_ptr error_fn, png_error_ptr warn_fn, png_voidp mem_ptr,
415:     png_malloc_ptr malloc_fn, png_free_ptr free_fn),PNG_ALLOCATED)
416: {
417:    png_struct create_struct;
418: #  ifdef PNG_SETJMP_SUPPORTED
419:       jmp_buf create_jmp_buf;
420: #  endif
421: 
422:    /* This temporary stack-allocated structure is used to provide a place to
423:     * build enough context to allow the user provided memory allocator (if any)
424:     * to be called.
425:     */
426:    memset(&create_struct, 0, (sizeof create_struct));
427: 
428: #  ifdef PNG_USER_LIMITS_SUPPORTED
429:       create_struct.user_width_max = PNG_USER_WIDTH_MAX;
430:       create_struct.user_height_max = PNG_USER_HEIGHT_MAX;
431: 
432: #     ifdef PNG_USER_CHUNK_CACHE_MAX
433:       create_struct.user_chunk_cache_max = PNG_USER_CHUNK_CACHE_MAX;
434: #     endif
435: 
436: #     if PNG_USER_CHUNK_MALLOC_MAX > 0 /* default to compile-time limit */
437:       create_struct.user_chunk_malloc_max = PNG_USER_CHUNK_MALLOC_MAX;
438: 
439:       /* No compile-time limit, so initialize to the system limit: */
440: #     elif defined PNG_MAX_MALLOC_64K /* legacy system limit */
441:       create_struct.user_chunk_malloc_max = 65536U;
442: 
443: #     else /* modern system limit SIZE_MAX (C99) */
444:       create_struct.user_chunk_malloc_max = PNG_SIZE_MAX;
445: #     endif
446: #  endif
447: 
448:    /* The following two API calls simply set fields in png_struct, so it is safe
449:     * to do them now even though error handling is not yet set up.
450:     */
451: #  ifdef PNG_USER_MEM_SUPPORTED
452:       png_set_mem_fn(&create_struct, mem_ptr, malloc_fn, free_fn);
453: #  else
454:       PNG_UNUSED(mem_ptr)
455:       PNG_UNUSED(malloc_fn)
456:       PNG_UNUSED(free_fn)
457: #  endif
458: 
459:    /* (*error_fn) can return control to the caller after the error_ptr is set,
460:     * this will result in a memory leak unless the error_fn does something
461:     * extremely sophisticated.  The design lacks merit but is implicit in the
462:     * API.
463:     */
464:    png_set_error_fn(&create_struct, error_ptr, error_fn, warn_fn);
465: 
466: #  ifdef PNG_SETJMP_SUPPORTED
467:       if (!setjmp(create_jmp_buf))
468: #  endif
469:       {
470: #  ifdef PNG_SETJMP_SUPPORTED
471:          /* Temporarily fake out the longjmp information until we have
472:           * successfully completed this function.  This only works if we have
473:           * setjmp() support compiled in, but it is safe - this stuff should
474:           * never happen.
475:           */
476:          create_struct.jmp_buf_ptr = &create_jmp_buf;
477:          create_struct.jmp_buf_size = 0; /*stack allocation*/
478:          create_struct.longjmp_fn = longjmp;
479: #  endif
480:          /* Call the general version checker (shared with read and write code):
481:           */
482:          if (png_user_version_check(&create_struct, user_png_ver) != 0)
483:          {
484:             png_structrp png_ptr = png_voidcast(png_structrp,
485:                 png_malloc_warn(&create_struct, (sizeof *png_ptr)));
486: 
487:             if (png_ptr != NULL)
488:             {
489:                /* png_ptr->zstream holds a back-pointer to the png_struct, so
490:                 * this can only be done now:
491:                 */
492:                create_struct.zstream.zalloc = png_zalloc;
493:                create_struct.zstream.zfree = png_zfree;
494:                create_struct.zstream.opaque = png_ptr;
495: 
496: #              ifdef PNG_SETJMP_SUPPORTED
497:                /* Eliminate the local error handling: */
498:                create_struct.jmp_buf_ptr = NULL;
499:                create_struct.jmp_buf_size = 0;
500:                create_struct.longjmp_fn = 0;
501: #              endif
502: 
503:                *png_ptr = create_struct;
504: 
505:                /* This is the successful return point */
506:                return png_ptr;
507:             }
508:          }
509:       }
510: 
511:    /* A longjmp because of a bug in the application storage allocator or a
512:     * simple failure to allocate the png_struct.
513:     */
514:    return NULL;
515: }

566: read_png(struct display *dp, struct buffer *bp, const char *operation,
567:    int transforms)
568: {
569:    png_structp pp;

571: 
572:    /* This cleans out any previous read and sets operation and transforms to
573:     * empty.
574:     */
575:    display_clean_read(dp);
576: 
577:    if (operation != NULL) /* else this is a verify and do not overwrite info */
578:    {
579:       dp->operation = operation;
580:       dp->transforms = transforms;
581:    }
582: 
583:    dp->read_pp = pp = png_create_read_struct(PNG_LIBPNG_VER_STRING, dp,
584:       display_error, display_warning);
585:    if (pp == NULL)
586:       display_log(dp, LIBPNG_ERROR, "failed to create read struct");
587: 
588:    /* The png_read_png API requires us to make the info struct, but it does the
589:     * call to png_read_info.
590:     */
591:    dp->read_ip = ip = png_create_info_struct(pp);
592:    if (ip == NULL)
593:       display_log(dp, LIBPNG_ERROR, "failed to create info struct");
594: 
595: #  ifdef PNG_SET_USER_LIMITS_SUPPORTED
596:       /* Remove the user limits, if any */
597:       png_set_user_limits(pp, 0x7fffffff, 0x7fffffff);
598: #  endif
599: 
600:    /* Set the IO handling */
601:    buffer_start_read(bp);
602:    png_set_read_fn(pp, bp, read_function);
603: 
604:    png_read_png(pp, ip, transforms, NULL/*params*/);

623: static void
624: update_display(struct display *dp)
625:    /* called once after the first read to update all the info, original_pp and
626:     * original_ip must have been filled in.
627:     */
628: {
629:    png_structp pp;
630:    png_infop   ip;
631: 
632:    /* Now perform the initial read with a 0 transform. */
633:    read_png(dp, &dp->original_file, "original read", 0/*no transform*/);
634: 
635:    /* Move the result to the 'original' fields */
636:    dp->original_pp = pp = dp->read_pp, dp->read_pp = NULL;
637:    dp->original_ip = ip = dp->read_ip, dp->read_ip = NULL;
638: 
639:    dp->original_rowbytes = png_get_rowbytes(pp, ip);
640:    if (dp->original_rowbytes == 0)
641:       display_log(dp, LIBPNG_BUG, "png_get_rowbytes returned 0");
642: 
643:    dp->chunks = png_get_valid(pp, ip, 0xffffffff);
644:    if ((dp->chunks & PNG_INFO_IDAT) == 0) /* set by png_read_png */
645:       display_log(dp, LIBPNG_BUG, "png_read_png did not set IDAT flag");
646: 
647:    dp->original_rows = png_get_rows(pp, ip);
648:    if (dp->original_rows == NULL)
649:       display_log(dp, LIBPNG_BUG, "png_read_png did not create row buffers");
650: 
651:    if (!png_get_IHDR(pp, ip,
652:       &dp->width, &dp->height, &dp->bit_depth, &dp->color_type,
653:       &dp->interlace_method, &dp->compression_method, &dp->filter_method))
654:       display_log(dp, LIBPNG_BUG, "png_get_IHDR failed");
655: 
656:    /* 'active' transforms are discovered based on the original image format;
657:     * running one active transform can activate others.  At present the code
658:     * does not attempt to determine the closure.
659:     */
660:    {
661:       png_uint_32 chunks = dp->chunks;
662:       int active = 0, inactive = 0;
663:       int ct = dp->color_type;
664:       int bd = dp->bit_depth;
665:       unsigned int i;
666: 
667:       for (i=0; i<TTABLE_SIZE; ++i)
668:       {
669:          if (transform_info[i].name != NULL)
670:          {
671:             int transform = transform_info[i].transform;
672: 
673:             if ((transform_info[i].valid_chunks == 0 ||
674:                   (transform_info[i].valid_chunks & chunks) != 0) &&
675:                (transform_info[i].color_mask_required & ct) ==
676:                   transform_info[i].color_mask_required &&
677:                (transform_info[i].color_mask_absent & ct) == 0 &&
678:                (transform_info[i].bit_depths & bd) != 0 &&
679:                (transform_info[i].when & TRANSFORM_R) != 0)
680:                active |= transform;
681: 
682:             else if ((transform_info[i].when & TRANSFORM_R) != 0)
683:                inactive |= transform;
684:          }
685:       }
686: 
687:       /* Some transforms appear multiple times in the table; the 'active' status
688:        * is the logical OR of these and the inactive status must be adjusted to
689:        * take this into account.
690:        */
691:       inactive &= ~active;
692: 
693:       dp->active_transforms = active;
694:       dp->ignored_transforms = inactive; /* excluding write-only transforms */
695:    }
696: }

698: static void
699: test_one_file(struct display *dp, const char *filename)
700: {
701:    /* First cache the file and update the display original file
702:     * information for the new file.
703:     */
704:    dp->operation = "cache file";
705:    dp->transforms = 0;
706:    display_cache_file(dp, filename);
707:    update_display(dp);
708: 
709:    /* First test: if there are options that should be ignored for this file
710:     * verify that they really are ignored.
711:     */
712:    if (dp->ignored_transforms != 0)
713:    {
714:       read_png(dp, &dp->original_file, "ignored transforms",
715:          dp->ignored_transforms);
716: 
717:       /* The result should be identical to the original_rows */
718:       if (!compare_read(dp, 0/*transforms applied*/))
719:          return; /* no point testing more */
720:    }
721: 
722: #ifdef PNG_WRITE_PNG_SUPPORTED
723:    /* Second test: write the original PNG data out to a new file (to test the
724:     * write side) then read the result back in and make sure that it hasn't
725:     * changed.
726:     */
727:    dp->operation = "write";
728:    write_png(dp, dp->original_ip, 0/*transforms*/);
729:    read_png(dp, &dp->written_file, NULL, 0/*transforms*/);
730:    if (!compare_read(dp, 0/*transforms applied*/))
731:       return;
732: #endif
733: 
734:    /* Third test: the active options.  Test each in turn, or, with the
735:     * EXHAUSTIVE option, test all possible combinations.
736:     */
737:    {
738:       /* Use unsigned int here because the code below to increment through all
739:        * the possibilities exhaustively has to use a compare and that must be
740:        * unsigned, because some transforms are negative on a 16-bit system.
741:        */
742:       unsigned int active = dp->active_transforms;
743:       int exhaustive = (dp->options & EXHAUSTIVE) != 0;
744:       unsigned int current = first_transform(active);
745:       unsigned int bad_transforms = 0;
746:       unsigned int bad_combo = ~0U;    /* bitwise AND of failing transforms */
747:       unsigned int bad_combo_list = 0; /* bitwise OR of failures */
748: 
749:       for (;;)
750:       {
751:          read_png(dp, &dp->original_file, "active transforms", current);
752:
